coredata.dc:title: Design and assessments on graded metal foam in heat storage tank: An experimental and numerical study
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Communications in Heat and Mass Transfer
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Xinyu
Wei, Pan
Yu, Jiabang
Huang, Xinyu
Yang, Xiaohu
Sundén, Bengt
coredata.dc:description: The effectiveness of solidification in latent heat storage (LHS) systems has been restricted by the low thermal conductivity of pure phase change materials (PCMs). To address this challenge, an innovative composite PCM impregnated with metal foam has been introduced. This study investigates a vertical thermal energy storage (TES unit) filling with foamed copper with radial gradient pore density, with a focus on enhancing energy storage and heat conduction mixing through natural convection. Experiments and numerical models are employed to research the variation of liquid fraction, solid-liquid interfaces, temperature field, and velocity field, as well as to assess heat release properties, including thermal transfer distribution and heat release quantity. Results indicate a 14.3% reduction in solidification duration for both positive and negative radially graded pore density arrangements, compared to a homogeneous structure. In addition, temperature uniformity is improved by 4.0% in these two optimization structures, due to the influence of varied pore density. Findings from this work offer guidance for building more efficient latent energy storage tanks.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Latent heat storage
Porous media
Gradient optimization
Pore density

head.ce:title: Design and assessments on graded metal foam in heat storage tank: An experimental and numerical study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of the Building Environment & Sustainability Technology, School of Human Settlements and Civil Engineering, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an 710049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of the Building Environment & Sustainability Technology, School of Human Settlements and Civil Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of the Building Environment & Sustainability Technology, School of Human Settlements and Civil Engineering, Xi'’an Jiaotong University, Xi'’an 710049, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China Northwest Architecture Design and Research Institute, CO. Ltd, Xi'an 710077, Shaanxi Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China Northwest Architecture Design and Research Institute, CO. Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: China Northwest Architecture Design and Research Institute, CO. Ltd, Xi'’an 710077, Shaanxi Province, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effectiveness of solidification in latent heat storage (LHS) systems has been restricted by the low thermal conductivity of pure phase change materials (PCMs). To address this challenge, an innovative composite PCM impregnated with metal foam has been introduced. This study investigates a vertical thermal energy storage (TES unit) filling with foamed copper with radial gradient pore density, with a focus on enhancing energy storage and heat conduction mixing through natural convection. Experiments and numerical models are employed to research the variation of liquid fraction, solid-liquid interfaces, temperature field, and velocity field, as well as to assess heat release properties, including thermal transfer distribution and heat release quantity. Results indicate a 14.3% reduction in solidification duration for both positive and negative radially graded pore density arrangements, compared to a homogeneous structure. In addition, temperature uniformity is improved by 4.0% in these two optimization structures, due to the influence of varied pore density. Findings from this work offer guidance for building more efficient latent energy storage tanks.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

mml:mtd.mml:mtext: Heat transfer coefficient between internal ligament

mml:math.mml:mtext: Effective thermal conductivity of metal foam
Liquid fraction in the metal foam

ce:section.ce:para: The present era witnesses the enormous progress of economy and technology, which has led to the emergence of energy shortage and environmental pollution. Consequently, sustainable energy development and energy structure improvement have become prime areas of investigation. Thermal storage technology facilitates efficient application of energy resources through optimizing the storage processes of intermittent renewable energy [1]. It utilizes heat storage materials to store energy and offers three primary means of heat storage: sensible heat storage (SHS) [2,3], latent heat storage (LHS) [4,5] also referred to as phase transition heat storage, and chemical heat storage (CHS) [6]. In comparison, SHS stores thermal energy directly through solid and liquid phases, with an easy implementation process, but suffers from challenges related to heat storage density and volume miniaturization owing to the thermal conductivity properties [7]. Conversely, LHS stores and releases energy via PCMs and boasts excellent energy storage capacity and stable temperatures on the phase transition process [8,9]. Nevertheless, low heat diffusivity of PCMs particularly impacts the velocity of energy charge and discharge [10,11]. CHS offers high heat storage density, but is expensive and commonly requires equipment with high technical specifications. In contrast, LHS presents a compromise solution with favorable storage costs, high storage density, and simple configurations. Numerous studies have reported on PCM material characterization, heat transfer structure design, and energy efficiency improvement for LHS [12–16].

Metallic foam, a sort of porous medium, offer excellent heat conductivity and large specific surface area advantages. Composite phase change materials (CPCMs) are manufactured through embedding phase change materials (PCMs) into metal foam, enhancing the solidification performance and phase change efficiency of PCMs [17]. An abundant body of experimental and simulative investigations have been conducted on the thermal process of enhanced phase transition in porous media. Yang et al. [18] exhibited that the filling of metallic foam enhances the melting/solidification efficiency of PCMs. Esapour et al. [19] employed a multi-tube as a designed LHS device to investigate the charge/discharge process of CPCMs using a local non-thermal equilibrium model. They examined the effects of HTF tube permutation distribution and the porosity of metallic foam during the phase transition course. Results revealed that adding the quantity of heat transfer tubes and packing porous medium improved the velocity of the melting/solidification course, while alterations in the arrangement of the thermal energy transfer tube had a minor effect on melting and solidification. Foamed copper with porosities of 0.7 and 0.9 can decrease the complete phase transition time by 55% and 14%, respectively. Mahdi et al. [20] established a mathematical model to investigate the influence of combining metal foam and nanofluids for the complete discharging time of the CPCMs. They reported that the combination resulted in a 94% reduction in total solidification time. Yang et al. [21] developed an innovative pin-fin metallic foam distribution and investigated the solidification process of PCM under varying combinations of pin fins and metal foams through experiments and numerical calculations. The research confirmed that the composite structure of 0.4 pin fin and 0.6 metallic foam minimized the discharging time. Mettawee et al. [22] utilized aluminum powder and paraffin as the CPCM and examined the role of metal particles in the heat storage process, suggesting that the total phase transition time reduced about 60% in comparison with pure paraffin, primarily during the solidification process. Nevertheless, the research did not explore the influence of metal particles on heat storage performance for the system. Zhang et al. [23] carried out a visualization test to explore the melting characteristics of the CPCM and discovered during the heat transfer process a local thermal imbalance between the paraffin and metal skeleton leading to a notable temperature discrepancy between the two. Additionally, the experimental results verified the simulation results. Asgari et al. [24] explored the performance of the physical characteristics of the branched fins and the volume ratio of the open-cell foam on the phase transition process, concluding that using thin fins and a 0.04 volume ratio of mixed Al2O3-Cu particles significantly increased the heat release rate of the PCM. Hosseinzadeh et al. [25] tried to investigate the evaluation of the heat release efficiency of the CPCMs concerning the addition of nanoparticles or fins to pure PCMs. They recommended that both methods can increase the solidification rate, but the optimized fin structure can better promote the solidification of the PCMs compared to adding nanoparticles.

The formation of a composite PCM (CPCM) presents varying heat conductivity depending on the material used to prepare the porous medium, structure, and proportion. Enhanced thermal conductivity leads to higher heat storage and release efficiency. Xiao et al. [26] constructed a visual experiment to examine the contribution of adding metal foam and molten salt during the charging procedure under high-temperature conditions. Results indicated that the charging time shortened by 74.0% with the addition of porous materials, and the porous materials significantly enhanced the mean volumetric powers in the melting process. Furthermore, Zhang et al. [27] prepared CPCMs by incorporating nickel and metal foam using a vacuum method. Resseach results exhibited that the embedded of foamed nickel increased the thermal diffusivity from 0.305 W∙m−1∙K−1 to 1.2 W∙m−1∙K−1 or about 3 times. In contrast, the filling of copper foam resulted in an improvement of up to roughly 4.9 W∙m−1∙K−1. In experiments conducted by Haillot et al. [28], expanded graphite of different densities were combined with PCMs, resulting in an optimization of the heat conductivity of PCMs by 5–100 times. Furthermore, Yang et al. [18] examined the impact of the fins, metallic foam and fins-metallic foam filled on the thermal energy storage process and the compared the solidification parameters with pure PCM. Shi et al. [29] utilized paraffin and starch-derived carbon foam in the fabrication of CPCM, studying the effects of changing percentages of carbon foam on the phase change procedure. Moreover, the comprehensive performance of the CPCM in terms of environmental protection, thermal conductivity, and chemical stability was also discussed. Li et al. [30] researched the impact of the volume fraction of PCM on the phase transition process in the CPCM formed by paraffin and aluminum foam. They also explored the performance of geometric distribution and roughness of the aluminum foam on heat conductivity enhancements.

The phase transition process in porous media is highly influenced by structural parameters, such as pore density. Zhong et al. [31] discovered that the heat diffusion coefficient of PCM would be added to 190, 270, 500, and 570 times by adding graphite foams with different pore diameters. Mancin et al. [32] conducted studies during the charging and discharging procedures of CPCMs by incorporating foamed material with a pore density of 5, 10, and 40, in respective. Research exhibited that the foamed metal remarkably elevated the thermal energy storage characteristics. Li et al. [33] explored the phase transition process of seven high-porosity copper foams filled with paraffin by conducting experiments and simulations. They analyzed the improvement of pore size on the temperature uniformity of CPCMs, proposed a numerical model of the non-thermal equilibrium, and verified the applicability of the model and the feasibility and necessity of applying the dual-energy equation. Tao et al. [34] utilized the LBM to examine the effects of pore size of foamed metal on the thermal energy storage characters of the CPCM, finding that as the pore size increased, the thermal conductivity enhanced while the influence of natural convection decreased. Dinesh et al. [35] investigated the performance of the pore distribution of the open-cell foam during the heat storage process, and revealed that the metallic foam with a smaller pore size was beneficial to phase transition efficiency. In another study, Yang et al. [36] proceeded a heat release experiment of the distilled water in a foamed copper with modified pore structure parameters, and discovered that a suitable gradient parameter optimization can ulterior strengthen the heat release process. Hu et al. [37] numerically explored the heat storage process of CPCM with graded structure at a pore scale level. It was documented that at a mean porosity of 0.90, the total phase transition time of the positive distribution was decreased by 15.5%, and for the negative model it was reduced by 2.6%. Ahmadi et al. [38] simulated the charging process of the graded CPCM. They argued that the optimal graded filling structure was found to reduce the heat storage time by 3.35%, while the total entropy increased by 7.96%.

In summary, the integration of metal foam utilizing in latent TES systems has been shown to significantly enhance thermal energy transfer efficiency. However, existing research has primarily focused on metallic foam with fixed pore structure parameters. Varying the pore density of open-cell foam can have different impacts on the phase transition process and further modify heat release efficiency. Currently, there is a knowledge gap in understanding the heat release process in the LHS unit filled with metallic foam with uneven pore parameters. Therefore, this study aims to design a LHS tank filled with graded metal foams, and experiments are carried out to test their contribution to solidification performance. Various gradients are designed, and transient solidification front visualizations are compared with numerical models. Comprehensive analysis of heat release performance is conducted, providing a better understanding and operation of the LHS system.

The TES tank exhibited in Fig. 1(a) is embedded with paraffin and foamed copper as the thermal energy storage medium. To gain a deeper understanding of the transient solidification process, one unit of the LHS tank is selected as the design target. Fig. 1(b) shows the TES unit made of organic glass, with dimensions of height (300 mm) × inner diameter (90 mm) × thickness (5 mm). A copper pipe, with dimensions of length (500 mm) × inner diameter (20 mm) × thickness (1 mm), is inserted in the medial of the LHS unit to facilitate circulating heat transfer fluid (HTF). Foamed copper with a pore density of 10 PPI, as displayed in Fig. 1(c), is combined with PCMs by the vacuum casting method. Furthermore, paraffin is served as the PCM in the experiment because of its favorable thermophysical properties and economic feasibility, with parameters summarized in Table 1 .

Temperature measurement points are typically arranged in the CPCM region to record temperature variations during the phase transition process. In this study, two thermocouples are distributed along the axial and radial directions inside the LHS unit. Point A is located at a height of 125 mm and located on the outer surface of the copper tube, while point B is at a height of 175 mm and positioned in the middle of the annular foamed copper (i.e., 25 mm away from the axis of symmetry of the HTF tube), which is depicted in Fig. 1(c). The experimental system is illustrated in Fig. 2 , consisting of three main modules: (1) HTF circulation module, which facilitates HTF circulation for the orderly conduct of the experiment and is equipped with a flowmeter (± 1.5%) to monitor flow rate; (2) Acquisition module, which collects temperature data from the thermocouples (± 0.2 °C) and tracks phase interface changes via a high-definition camera lens; and (3) Solidification experiment module, which is linked with other modules through pipelines and wrapped with 50 mm thick insulation cotton to reduce heat loss.

Fig. 2 depicts the experimental system constructed for visualizing the heat storage/release unit consisting of a transparent concentric shell-and-tube construction with the inner tube diameter of 20 mm and outer shell diameter of 90 mm. To gather temperature data of the CPCM, an Agilent 34970A data collecting instrument is utilized, and temperature calibration employs T-type thermocouples (Omega CL3515R). A 40-million-pixel high-definition camera is employed to capture phase interfaces. In order to establish consistent initial experimental conditions, preparatory work is completed before the operation. Initially, the heating condition of the hot-water bath is set to 80 °C, with valves 4 and 6 open, circulating HTF in the experimental pipes. Meanwhile, the value of the cold-water bath is set to 30 °C, with valve 2 open for cycling cold fluid to ensure HTF temperature stability. Once the TES unit is completely melted and internal temperature approaches consistency, and the cold-water bath meets requirements, valves 4 and 6 and the hot-water bath are closed. Valve 2 is closed, valve 1 and 3 are opened, the cold bath external circulation is activated, and flow rate is adjusted to 5.65 L·min-1 via valve 1 to initiate the heat release experiment. The thermocouple collects temperature information per ten seconds, and the camera records phase interfaces simultaneously. The air conditioner is turned on to maintain the experimental ambient temperature at 26 °C.

The total uncertainty in this study includes the uncertainty of thermocouple measurement δT, the uncertainty of flowmeter measurement δϕ, the uncertainty of the thickness of the copper foam specimen δh, the uncertainty of foamed copper pore characters δε, and the uncertainty of the hot-water and cold-water bath (δT hot and δT cold). The analysis of the uncertainties is exhibited in Table 2 . The uncertainty of the experiments is expressed in Eq. (1), which is estimated to 6.86%.
(1)
δ =
δT / T
2
+
δϕ / ϕ
2
+
δh / h
2
+
δε / ε
2
+
δ
T hot
/
T hot
2
+
δ
T cold
/
T cold
2

The phase change heat accumulator contains several tube bundles, and to establish a general rule, this study focuses on the vertical TES unit, as displayed in Fig. 3(a). It serves to show that the changes in all directions of the TES unit on the solidification course are similar, allowing for the simplification of the TES unit in a two-dimensional axisymmetric model, where the axis of symmetry is on the left side as demonstrated. The height L of the unit is 270 mm, with an inner tube diameter of r = 20 mm, and an outer shell diameter of 90 mm. Additionally, a novel filling structure is proposed, which divides the unit into three radial regions with varying pore density, as illustrated in Fig. 3(c). The copper foam pore parameters in the filling region are arranged to constitute a gradient distribution (positive, uniform, and negative gradients) in the radial directions. Three composite PCM (CPCM) configurations explored in the simulation are summarized in Table 3 . Numerical simulation results for various gradient structures are compared to evaluate the optimization of the structures.
Synthesizing the above assumptions, a numerical model of the fluid thermal energy exchange zone and PCM phase transition process is constituted.

In the formulas above: presents the external mean value of a certain number in the control volume; ρ f, μf, λ fe, c p,f, and L account for the density, dynamic viscosity, thermal conductivity, specific heat and latent heat value of PCM, in respective; σ reveals the liquid fraction in CPCM region, σ = ε
f f
; ρ s, c ps, λ se, ε, and a sf donate the density, specific heat, effective thermal conductivity and specific surface area of porous media; γ is the heat expansion coefficient; K exhibits the permeability; C E is defined as the inertial coefficient; λ td presents the thermal dispersion coefficient of thermal conductivity.

is expressed as the Forchheimer-Darcy term，which is employed to explain the viscous resistance and inertial resistance in the porous media. A m is an additional source term of the momentum equation:

In this formula: δ donates the empirical constant, which is 10−4; ψ exhibits the coefficient of the mushy zone, taken as 108. The liquid fraction f m is defined by the following formula (15):

In the expressions, f m is the fraction of liquid phase; T PCM, T solidus, and T liquidus respectively exhibit the temperature, solidification and melting temperature of the PCM, in respective. When f m = 0, A m tends to infinity, and the value of δ ensures there are no errors in the calculation.

The pore size d p, fiber diameter d f, and specific surface area a sf are defined as [39]:

In the formula: D donates the ligament thickness, which is confirmed by experimental measurements.

The non-thermal equilibrium model is required to consider the effective HEAT conductivity of PCM and open-cell foamed metal [41], thermal diffusivity λ td [42]. The corresponding formulas are as follows:

In these formulas: c d exhibits the drag coefficient, when the porosity is within 0.8991–0.978, the value is 1.56 [43]; χ donates the flow tortuosity; G accounts for the shape function of foamed copper skeleton, which is represented by the following formulas [44]:

The governing equation of transient heat and mass transfer involving phase transition and moving boundary is calculated utilizing the finite volume method. The PISO algorithm is employed to solve the pressure and velocity coupling effects of forced convection in HTF and local natural convection in liquid PCM. The PRESTO! format is used for pressure correction, and other physical quantities are discretized by the second-order upwind method. Meanwhile, the relaxation factors of momentum, pressure correction, energy and liquid fraction are setting as 0.6, 0.3, 0.8 and 0.7 for assembling the convergence of continuity, momentum and energy eqs. (10−6, 10−6 and 10−8).

The calculation time for numerical calculations is directly proportional to the number of grids, which means a higher number of grids leads to longer calculation times. The relationship between the accuracy of numerical calculations and the number of grids is complex, as with a higher number of grids, greater rounding-off errors occur due to computer storage, and it is not necessarily true that more accurate results are obtained. Therefore, selecting a reasonable number of grids is critical for obtaining accurate numerical calculation results. This study considers three sets of grids for the two-dimensional model and compares and analyzes the variation curves of the measuring points, which are displayed in Fig. 4 . With an increasing number of grids, the difference between the three sets of grids is below 1.5%. To maintain a reasonable error range, the number of grids for the two-dimensional case is chosen as 51,852.

The heat release process of PCMs is a transient problem, and calculation results for this problem depend not only on mesh quality and quantity but also on the calculation time step. A time step that is too large can negatively affect calculation accuracy while also leading to longer calculation times. To address this concern, three sets of time steps are chosen to calculate and present temperature at the measuring points as exhibited in Fig. 5 . The time step size independence verification of the grid reveals that the maximum difference is 0.34%. To maintain a reasonable error range in simulations, the medium time step size is chosen.

To verify simulation results, this study compares the temperatures of points A and B. As depicted in Fig. 6 , the curve changing tendencies of the numerical and experimental data are consistent, and the two curves basically coincide. As the measuring point passes through the liquid phase sensible heat release period, the phase transition latent heat release period, and the solid phase sensible heat release period, the temperature curve eventually levels off. The average relative error of measuring point A and point B are 3.19% and 4.04%, in respective. Within a reasonable error range, the numerical simulation model can be considered accurate. To detailed verify the accuracy of the simulated model, this study compares the phase interfaces simulated at four different time points with experimental phase interfaces selected for comparison, as displayed in Fig. 7 . During the heat release process, the phase transition appearance initiates from the upper zone of the CPCM region because of the lower temperature of the TES unit. Results suggest that the degree of agreement between the simulation and verification phase interfaces is extremely high.

The impact of different radial gradient pore density structures during solidification process are investigated by Case1-N (75–45-15), Case2-U (45–45-45) and Case3-P (15–45-75), which are summarized in Table 3. As demonstrated in Fig. 8 , this study displays curves that reflect variations with time during the heat release process of the liquid fraction (f m) and enhancement rate (Er). The liquid fraction reveals the melting paraffin share in the total PCM, while the enhancement rate represents the relative growth rate of the remaining structure combination based on uniform filling time. These values are illustrated in Eq. (24).
(24)
Er
t
=
f m
t
−
f base
t
/
f base
t

As demonstrated, there is little difference between the Case1-N, Case2-U and Case3-P structures within the first 400 s. However, after this point, differences gradually emerge, with solidification rates of the positive and negative gradient structures consistent, requiring only 3600 s to complete the solidification process. Complete solidification times of the Case1-N and Case3-P structures have the similar variation tendency and are shortened by 14.3% compared to the uniform gradient structure. This is because inhomogeneous pore density strengthens the natural convection between different gradient layers, and has little effect on the thermal conductivity. The results providing evidence that there is little difference in the optimization effect of natural convection in positive and negative radial gradient structures, and both radially graded pore density arrangements can enhance solidification efficiency.

Fig. 9 shows the solidification performance of CPCM1, CPCM2, and CPCM3 in the solidification process over time. The discussion suggests that the solidification process of Case1-N and Case3-P is similar. Results are further verified by the liquid fraction of the layers, revealing negligible discrepancies between the positive and negative distributions during solidification processes in each region, with both being faster than the uniform gradient. Differences are more significant in the CPCM3 region. Regarding thermal conductivity, the three structures filled with metal foam are the same, indicating that natural convection plays a decisive part in thermal energy utilizing efficiency enhancement by gradient pore density.

Phase interface evolution contours of solidification at 200 s, 1000 s, 1800 s, 2600 s, and 3500 s are selected to assess changes in liquid phase fraction curves, as shown in Fig. 10 . Within the first 200 s of solidification, a slight bulge appears at the phase interfaces in the lower region of Case3-P. However, with the gradual solidification of the paraffin closed to the HTF tube, the heat release process is promoted by thermal conduction, resulting in a gradual decrease in the bulge. After 1000 s, it appears that the upside zone of the CPCM region is adjacent to the cold fluid inlet section, and the difference is larger at the same time. Consequently, the upper region completes the solidification process first, followed by gradually decreasing phase interfaces. Moreover, the phase interfaces of the Case1-N and Case3-P structures evolve slightly faster, as uneven pore density structures enhance natural convection. After solidification for 2600 s, the phase interfaces of the three structures show no significant difference, indicating that CPCMs mainly rely on heat conduction to transfer cold during the later solidification stages.

Fig. 11 exhibits the temperature change tendency of the three measuring points in the CPCM region during solidification over time. The measured temperature curves of Case1-N and Case2-P during the solidification process are almost entirely coincident, indicating no significant difference in the local magnified images, which supports previous results. The temperature curves of both structures at the same location change faster than those of the uniform gradient structure. Due to stronger natural convection on top of the CPCMs, measuring point A completes the phase change and begins to take advantage of the sensible heat energy. The positive and negative gradient structures complete latent heat release in approximately 3590 s, whereas the uniform gradient requires 4140 s. These findings demonstrate that the modified structures shorten the time needed by 13.3%.

Fig. 12 demonstrates the variation tendency of the temperature field contours on the solidification process over time, with time nodes selection consistent with the phase interface contours. At the primary solidification stage, the isotherm is nearly parallel to the HTF tube. There is a natural convective disturbance at the lower part of the positive gradient, bending in the forward direction of the x coordinate. As solidification progresses in the CPCM1 region and most areas of the TES unit, and reaches the phase change temperature, thermal conduction takes up most of the role in the solidification procedure. In addition, the outer isotherm advances with the solidification, and the upper region bends in the forward direction of the x coordinate on account of its proximity to the cold fluid inlet. After 3500 s of solidification, the 52 °C isotherm of the Case1-N intersects the outer wall surface of the TES unit, while the positive gradient intersects both the outer and bottom of the TES unit. This phenomenon occurs mainly in the later stage, accounting for the natural convection influence on the positive distribution of the CPCM1 region in the initial stages.

In addition to heat release performance, the long-term security and operational stability of TES systems are important reference indicators. The internal temperature field of the TES unit characterizes the heat diffusion capacity of different structures. This study uses hourly temperature uniformity to assess the temperature field optimization of the TES unit, as demonstrated in Eq. (25). The mean temperature uniformity is determined by calculate the average uniformity integral, as displayed in Eq. (26):
(25)
σ
t
=
1 / n
∑
i = 1
n
T i
x y z t
−
T mean
t
2
(26)
σ ¯
t
=
∫ 0
t max
σ
t
dt /
t max
where T i donates the temperature of location i; T mean reveals the average temperature of TES unit; x, y and z respectively present the x, y and z coordinates; t max exhibits the maximum average integration time.

The temperature uniformity curves over time during solidification are shown in Fig. 13 . Between 820 and 3440 s, strong local natural convection disturbance inside Case1-N and Case3-P results in local high temperature, leading to a temperature deviation larger than that of the uniform gradient structure. After solidification for 3440 s, the phase change of positive and negative gradients is essentially completed. Thermal conduction promotes sensible thermal energy release, and the temperature difference inside the CPCM gradually decreases, with temperature uniformity gradually increasing. Based on the non-gradient distribution, the temperature uniformity of Case1-N and Case3-P is enhanced by 4.0% during the solidification process.

Fig. 14 illustrates contours of the velocity field variation on the solidification process. Based on the solidification temperature field shown in Fig. 12, velocity streamline fields are drawn at 50 s, 100 s, 200 s, 300 s, and 500 s. After 500 s, most areas of the CPCM region have achieved solidification temperature, and natural convection is almost non-existent. The negative gradient of flow concentration areas is concentrated in the CPCM3, while the Case3-P structure has a greater speed in the CPCM1. The non-gradient distribution, without variation in the pore density, has a vortex core concentrated in the middle region of the CPCM, consistent with the height of the solidification front. Due to the diminutive pore density in the positive structure CPCM1, the velocity field rotates counterclockwise, and heat in the lower area transfers to the inlet section through the outer circulation, creating a large temperature difference. The heat exchange performance in the inlet section is therefore stronger than in the lower area. Consequently, the solidification speed of the lower zone of the CPCM region is slightly speedy than that of the upside zone. However, the lower zone can provide limited cold, and the upside zone of the CPCM region is sufficiently cold through thermal conduction, resulting in faster phase interface movement.

The heat flux value of the heat transfer wall presents the quantity of thermal energy exchange between the fluid and the PCM, with a negative value indicating that the PCM transfers heat to the HTF. The heat release capacity characterizes the heat release speed of the TES unit, and the calculation method is expressed as:
(27)
Q =
c
p , f
m PCM
β
T ¯
liquid
t
−
T 0
+
c
p , s
m PCM
1 − β
T ¯
solid
t
−
T 0
+
m PCM
βL
where Q exhibits the solidification capacity;
T ¯
liquid
and
T ¯
solid
presents the main temperature of melting and solidification regions;
m PCM
donates the quality of PCM.

Fig. 15(a) exhibits the curves of heat flux on the heat exchange wall over time. At the primary stage of solidification, the variation of the three structures rises rapidly, then enters the plateau stage, representing a phase transition process with comparatively minor temperature and little change in heat flux. Within 1000 s of solidification, heat flux curves of the three structures are largely similar, implying that the temperature difference is consistent due to unchanged thermal conductivity. After 1000 s of solidification, the curves of Case1-N and Case3-P increase. While cases with the same thermal conductivity indicate that the area-mean temperature in the CPCM region under the positive and negative gradient distributions is higher than that of Case2-U. Under the action of optimizing structure, natural convection in the macro-porous zone enhances solidification efficiency. Fig. 15(b) demonstrates the changing curves of heat storage with time, supporting that the thermal energy release rate of Case1-N and Case3-P structures is faster than the rate of the no-gradient distribution.

This paper simulates the impact of varying gradient foamed copper distributions during the solidification process and evaluates the performance of the LHS unit through indicators including phase interface, temperature, velocity, and heat flow. The gradient optimization structure is found to effectively improve the heat release effect. The following are the main conclusions of this study:
(1)
The solidification rates of positive and negative distributions exhibit a consistent trend for the effects of gradient pore density, reducing complete solidification time by 14.3%.
(2)
The gradient pore structure is found to ameliorate the uniformity of temperature field and result in a 4.0% uniform temperature field for both positive and negative distributions compared with uniform gradient structure.
(3)
The natural convection in the macro-porous zone enhances solidification efficiency under the action of optimizing pore density structure, since the exothermic efficiency of both positive and negative distributions are promoted.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51976155), Key Scientific and Technological Innovation Team of Shaanxi Province (2023-CX-TD-29), and the K. C. Wong Education Foundation.
